title: Overpass, round 19: rotate on the B noise, or hold until it's confirmed?

source.note: MOCK DATA — hand-authored fixture. A real deployment builds scenarios from the signed-in user's tracked stats via debrief-fetch-cs2-stats (or, later, demo parsing); numbers below are realistic but not from a real match.

presented_to_user.role: You are the in-game leader on the CT side.
presented_to_user.situation: Overpass, round 19, you lead 12–6. Both teams full-buy. You are set up 2-1-2: two B (you and your AWPer), one connector, two A (long and site). At 1:25 on the round clock, smokes and an incendiary land at B — monster and the site entrance — and you hear one flash. Your B partner calls 'they're coming B, rotate someone'. But you have seen no bodies, no bomb, and heard no footsteps closer than monster. All half the Ts have been defaulting until 1:10–1:00 and hitting late. Your connector player reports it's quiet mid. You have to call it now: pull a player from A to stack B, or hold the 2-1-2 until you get a confirm.
known_facts: Utility seen at B so far: two smokes and one incendiary, one flash heard. A committed B execute from this team has averaged 5–6 pieces of utility in this match; their fakes have averaged 2–3.
No bodies and no bomb have been spotted at B. The bomb was last seen mid-T spawn on your connector player's early peek.
This T side's timing pattern all half: default until 1:10–1:00, then hit. It is currently 1:25 — 15+ seconds earlier than every real hit they have thrown.
Rotating your A-long player to B takes ~13 seconds; rotating him back takes the same. A 1-3-1 A side versus a full A execute this match has lost the site before rotations arrive twice.
Your AWPer holds the monster angle — a real B contact must cross his crosshair. He has opening-picked 4 of 6 B hits this match.
You have won both previous retakes at A this map when the site was held 2-strong at contact; you have won 0 of 2 when it was held 1-strong.
presented_to_user.question: Do you pull from A to answer the B utility now, or hold the 2-1-2 until you see a body or the bomb? Explain your call — what are you weighing, what are you betting on, and what would make you wrong?

ground_truth.actual_decision: The call was to hold the 2-1-2 and demand a confirm: nobody rotated on the utility alone; the connector player shaded one step toward B heaven for a faster answer IF a body appeared, without leaving mid.
ground_truth.actual_outcome: It was a fake. The real hit came at A 14 seconds later — four Ts and the bomb through long with a full utility set. Because both A players were still home, the long player got the opening kill off his one-way smoke, the site 2v4 held long enough for the connector and one B player to arrive, and the round ended 12–7 CT (4v2 retake never materialized; the T hit died on entry). The utility read was the tell: 2–3 pieces matches this team's established fake pattern, 5–6 their real executes — and no bomb, no bodies, 15 seconds ahead of their every real hit this half. Post-round, holding was modeled at ~68% win; the early B rotate (leaving A 1-strong against the real hit) at ~35%, consistent with this map's own 0-for-2 record on 1-strong site holds.
decision_rationale_factors: Utility accounting: this team's own in-match pattern — fakes average 2–3 pieces, executes 5–6 — made the B utility a coin the data had already weighted toward fake.
Confirm hierarchy: bodies and bomb outrank noise. Smoke and fire cost the Ts 900 credits to throw; your rotation costs a 13-second one-way trip that the real hit is designed to spend.
Timing tells: every real hit this half came after 1:10; utility at 1:25 is out of pattern for an execute and in pattern for buying rotation-bait time.

The asymmetry of the error: over-rotating loses the A site outright (0-of-2 on 1-strong holds), while under-rotating still leaves a 2v-few B contact that the monster AWP (4-of-6 opening picks) is specifically positioned to stall until help arrives.
Cheap insurance existed that wasn't a rotation: shading the connector player one step toward heaven cut the response time to a REAL B hit without surrendering mid or A.
ground_truth.defensible_alternative: Rotating early is defensible IF it is argued from the score state as risk management rather than from the noise: at 12–6 with a big lead, paying a small equity cost to never lose a site to a fast B execute is a coherent 'don't lose the winnable map' line — especially if the user prices what they're giving up at A, sends the CLOSEST player (connector, not A-long), and commits to rotating back on an A confirm. A user who rotates because 'utility means they're coming' — reading the noise as information the utility count contradicts — is where it stops being defensible.
common_mistakes: Treating utility as a confirm: rotating on smoke and fire with no bodies and no bomb is answering 900 credits of bait with the exact 13-second trip it was thrown to buy.
Ignoring the team's own in-match patterns sitting in the facts — the fake/execute utility counts and the timing tell.
Rotating the WRONG player: pulling A-long (the 13-second trip that breaks the 2-strong A hold) instead of shading the connector.
Symmetric-risk thinking: treating over- and under-rotation as equal errors when the map's own record (0-of-2 on 1-strong holds, AWP 4-of-6 at monster) says they are not.
No falsifier — never naming the confirm (body, bomb, or a third+ utility piece) that would flip them to a full rotation.

verified_data_points.note: MOCK values, internally consistent. Not from a real match.

utilread: the utility-count read against this team's own fake and execute patterns
confirm: the confirm hierarchy — bodies and bomb over noise
timing: the timing tell against this half's real hits
asymmetry: the asymmetry of the two errors, priced from this map's own numbers
insurance: the cheap insurance option that isn't a full rotation

missing_priority: the utility accounting — 2–3 pieces matches their fakes, 5–6 their executes, and you saw three
the confirm hierarchy: no bodies, no bomb — smoke and fire are 900 credits of noise
the error asymmetry: over-rotating loses A outright (0-of-2 on 1-strong holds); under-rotating still leaves the monster AWP stalling
the timing tell — utility at 1:25 is 15+ seconds out of pattern for this team's real hits
an explicit falsifier — the confirm (body, bomb, more utility) that flips you to a full rotation
key_insights: The tell was already quantified in the facts: this team fakes with 2–3 pieces and executes with 5–6 — the B utility was a weighted coin, not a mystery.
The two errors are not the same size: rotating early loses a site outright, holding late still leaves a positioned AWP and a 13-second response — price the asymmetry, not just the fear.
Rotations should be bought with confirms, not noise — smoke and fire cost the Ts 900 credits precisely because your 13-second trip is worth more.
mock_synth.covering_decision_fb: Clear call to rotate and answer the B utility — the opposite of the winning call, but committed and actionable. Capped slightly because the utility count, the timing, and the missing bomb all pointed at the fake.
mock_synth.matched_decision_fb: Clear call to hold the setup and demand a confirm — refusing to spend a rotation on noise is exactly the disciplined, evidence-led call this dimension rewards.
mock_synth.risk_hint: the one time the low-utility look is real and the B pair loses the site waiting for a confirm, or a slow answer if the bomb does appear at B
